coredata.dc:title: Pd/CQDs/TiO2–NH2 composite schottky catalyst for efficient hydrogen production from formic acid dehydrogenation under visible light
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Fengqiu
Wang, Qiu
Lin, Shengda
Luo, Huanhu
Yu, Wanjin
Liu, Wucan
Cheng, Dang-guo
coredata.dc:description: The development of photocatalytic materials with good adsorption capacity and high visible light activity is essential for efficient and stable photocatalytic hydrogen production from formic acid dehydrogenation. The wide band gap of TiO2 photocatalyst materials limits their application in the field of visible light catalysis. In this work, a new composite material, Pd/CQDs/TiO2–NH2, is developed. Here, Pd is attached to amine-functionalized mesoporous titanium dioxide modified with carbon quantum dots (CQDs). This configuration is designed to enhance the catalytic activity for formic acid dehydrogenation when exposed to visible light. The Pd/CQDs-1/TiO2–NH2 catalyst exhibited outstanding performance under visible light, achieving a turnover frequency (TOF) of 2666.1 h−1 at 308 K and perfect hydrogen selectivity. Compared to the Pd/TiO2–NH2 catalyst with a TOF of 1715.5 h−1, this is a significant improvement. The data implies that the addition of CQDs significantly increases light efficiency, aids in the separation of photogenerated charge carriers, and enhances hydrogen production. Ultimately, the experiments clarified how the combined action of Pd, CQDs, and TiO2–NH2 synergistically boosts the catalytic dehydrogenation of formic acid under visible light. The findings present an innovative approach to improving Pd/TiO2 schottky materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Formic acid
Palladium
Carbon quantum dots
Amine modification
Photocatalytic hydrogen evolution

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a,b) TEM image and particle size distribution of CQDs; (c) STEM image of CQDs-1/TiO2; (d,e) STEM image of Pd/CQDs-1/TiO2–NH2 and element distribution image (inset: size distribution of Pd nanoparticles).

(a) XRD profiles of Pd/TiO2–NH2 alongside different Pd/CQDs/TiO2–NH2 variants; (b) Infrared spectra for TiO2, CQDs/TiO2, and Pd/CQDs-1/TiO2–NH2; (c) Nitrogen adsorption and desorption curves for Pd/TiO2–NH2 and Pd/CQDs-1/TiO2–NH2; (d) Curves showing pore size distribution for Pd/TiO2–NH2 and Pd/CQDs-1/TiO2–NH2.

(a) Survey scans from XPS for Pd/CQDs-1/TiO2–NH2; (b) Carbon 1s spectra of CQDs-1/TiO2, CQDs-1/TiO2–NH2, and Pd/CQDs-1/TiO2–NH2; (c) Nitrogen 1s spectra for CQDs-1/TiO2, CQDs-1/TiO2–NH2, Pd/CQDs-1/TiO2–NH2, and Pd/TiO2–NH2; (d) Palladium 3d spectra for Pd/TiO2–NH2 and Pd/CQDs-1/TiO2–NH2.

(a,b) DRS data and Tauc plots for Pd/TiO2–NH2, Pd/CQDs-0.5/TiO2–NH2, Pd/CQDs-1/TiO2–NH2, and Pd/CQDs-2/TiO2–NH2; (c) Mott-Schottky analysis for Pd/CQDs-1/TiO2–NH2 and Pd/TiO2–NH2; (d–f) EIS Nyquist plots, current-time (I-t) curves, and PL spectra for Pd/TiO2–NH2 and Pd/CQDs-1/TiO2–NH2.

(a) Volume of gases (H2+CO2) released by different catalysts versus time under light conditions, (b–c) The volume of gas versus time curves and TOF for the formic acid dehydrogenation reaction of Pd/CQDs-1/TiO2–NH2 and Pd/TiO2–NH2 under light and dark conditions, (d) Cycling test of Pd/CQDs-1/TiO2–NH2, where TOF is calculated from data at 10 min into the reaction, TOF = mmolgas/(mmolPd × h).
(a) TEM image of Pd/CQDs-1/TiO2–NH2 after four FA dehydrogenation cycles, (b) size distribution of Pd nanoparticles in recycled Pd/CQDs-1/TiO2–NH2.

(a) The volume of gas versus time graph for Pd/CQDs-1/TiO2–NH2 catalyzed dehydrogenation of aqueous FA/SF (1:1) solution under light in the presence and absence of NaOH trap ([NaOH] = 10.0 M); (b) Gas chromatogram graph of the gases produced by the dehydrogenation reaction of 2 wt% Pd/TiO2–NH2 catalyst in FA/SF (1:1) solution compared with pure CO.

(a) Illustration showing the mechanism of hydrogen generation from formic acid by Pd/CQDs-1/TiO2–NH2 under visible light exposure. (b) Band structure representations for the prepared Pd/TiO2–NH2 and Pd/CQDs-1/TiO2–NH2 samples.
Schematic of the detailed stages of creating Pd/CQDs/TiO2–NH2.
Pore structure parameters of Pd/TiO2–NH2, Pd/CQDs-0.5/TiO2–NH2, Pd/CQDs-1/TiO2–NH2 and Pd/CQDs-2/TiO2–NH2.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The surface area of the sample was calculated by BET model using adsorption data.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The pore size and pore volume of the sample were calculated by the BJH model using the adsorption isotherm branch.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Data obtained via ICP-OES for various Pd/CQDs/TiO2–NH2 catalysts.
Electrical properties of Pd/CQDs-1/TiO2–NH2 and Pd/TiO2–NH2.
The initial TOF values for formic acid decomposition when using different Pd-based catalysts.

head.ce:title: Pd/CQDs/TiO2–NH2 composite schottky catalyst for efficient hydrogen production from formic acid dehydrogenation under visible light

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemical and Biological Engineering, Zhejiang Provincial Key Laboratory of Advanced Chemical Engineering Manufacture Technology, Zhejiang University, Hangzhou, 310027, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemical and Biological Engineering
Zhejiang Provincial Key Laboratory of Advanced Chemical Engineering Manufacture Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemical and Biological Engineering, Zhejiang Provincial Key Laboratory of Advanced Chemical Engineering Manufacture Technology, Zhejiang University, Hangzhou, 310027, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Fluorinated Greenhouse Gases Replacement and Control Treatment, Zhejiang Research Institute of Chemical Industry, Hangzhou, 310032, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Fluorinated Greenhouse Gases Replacement and Control Treatment
Zhejiang Research Institute of Chemical Industry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Fluorinated Greenhouse Gases Replacement and Control Treatment, Zhejiang Research Institute of Chemical Industry, Hangzhou 310032, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of photocatalytic materials with good adsorption capacity and high visible light activity is essential for efficient and stable photocatalytic hydrogen production from formic acid dehydrogenation. The wide band gap of TiO2 photocatalyst materials limits their application in the field of visible light catalysis. In this work, a new composite material, Pd/CQDs/TiO2–NH2, is developed. Here, Pd is attached to amine-functionalized mesoporous titanium dioxide modified with carbon quantum dots (CQDs). This configuration is designed to enhance the catalytic activity for formic acid dehydrogenation when exposed to visible light. The Pd/CQDs-1/TiO2–NH2 catalyst exhibited outstanding performance under visible light, achieving a turnover frequency (TOF) of 2666.1 h−1 at 308 K and perfect hydrogen selectivity. Compared to the Pd/TiO2–NH2 catalyst with a TOF of 1715.5 h−1, this is a significant improvement. The data implies that the addition of CQDs significantly increases light efficiency, aids in the separation of photogenerated charge carriers, and enhances hydrogen production. Ultimately, the experiments clarified how the combined action of Pd, CQDs, and TiO2–NH2 synergistically boosts the catalytic dehydrogenation of formic acid under visible light. The findings present an innovative approach to improving Pd/TiO2 schottky materials.

ce:section.ce:para: Severe energy crises and environmental damage have driven humans to shift the global energy landscape from traditional fossil fuels to green and sustainable alternatives in order to reach the goals of the Paris Agreement [1–3]. Hydrogen (H2), valued for its high energy density and extensive availability, has become a focal point of research due to its role as a clean, renewable energy source with minimal environmental impact [4–6]. The hydrogen energy field continues to struggle with the major issue of devising a reliable and efficient storage solution for hydrogen [7–9]. Liquid Organic Hydrogen Carriers (LOHCs) offer a practical and effective approach to addressing the hydrogen storage challenges mentioned above [10–12]. Formic acid (FA) stands out as an efficient hydrogen transport medium because it is non-toxic, contains a significant amount of hydrogen (4.4 wt% or 53 g/L), is easy to handle, and has a simple hydrogen release mechanism [13–15]. Additionally, FA can be produced directly from hydrogen and carbon dioxide, highlighting its eco-friendly characteristics as a medium for hydrogen storage [16,17]. The hydrogen release from FA (HCOOH → H2 + CO2) is achieved through dehydrogenation. However, managing the catalyst is crucial to prevent contamination from carbon monoxide, which can result from the competing dehydration process (HCOOH → H2O + CO) [18–20]. Consequently, there is a need to engineer a catalyst that excels in hydrogen production from FA under gentle conditions, ensuring robust performance and minimal deactivation.

So far, catalysts based on Pd [21–24], Pd-Au [25–28], Pd-Ag [29–35], and Pd–Ir [36–38] nanoparticles (NPs) have proven to be particularly effective for producing hydrogen from FA under relatively mild conditions. Smaller metal nanoclusters (NCs), due to their increased number of edge and corner atoms and superior surface-to-volume ratios compared to nanoparticles (NPs), exhibit heightened activity in diverse uses. Despite their advantages, metal NCs are susceptible to significant aggregation during reactions due to their elevated surface energy. This aggregation can result in reduced catalytic performance and shorter lifespan [39,40]. Using appropriate supports to immobilize and spread metal NCs mitigates their tendency to aggregate and boosts their reaction efficiency through the strong metal-support interaction (SMSI). TiO2 has three phases of anatase, rutile and plate titanium, all of which have a wide band gap of 3.0–3.2 eV [41–43]. Therefore, TiO2 can only absorb ultraviolet light (UV), which only accounts for a very small part of sunlight, and the visible light, which accounts for about 43% of sunlight, cannot be absorbed and utilized. For effective reaction performance, it is crucial that a photocatalyst can capture light effectively, as this directly influences the generation of charge carriers required for the process. Therefore, increasing the light absorption of TiO2 is an effective way to improve its photocatalytic activity. Although previous work improved some of the absorption of visible light by introducing Pd nanoparticles, the overall photocatalytic activity of the catalyst under visible light irradiation is still very limited. In order to improve absorption of sunlight for TiO2, various strategies have been developed, such as doping metal or nonmetallic elements, introducing cocatalysts, adopting heterojunction structures, etc. To boost photocatalytic efficiency in the visible light spectrum, integrating TiO2 with responsive materials like semiconductors, carbon substances, or metallic elements to form heterojunctions proves highly effective [44–46]. Newly emerging as zero-dimensional (0D) carbon materials, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have garnered significant interest for their stability in chemical environments, impressive light interaction capabilities, and efficient electrical conduction [47,48]. Due to the upconversion and photoluminescence characteristics of CQDs [49,50] they can serve as photosensitizers under long-wavelength light irradiation, enhancing light utilization efficiency and, subsequently, the photocatalytic activity of the catalyst. Therefore, CQDs can be used as photosensitizers or cocatalysts in photocatalytic applications. Hu et al. [51] reported a CQDs modified TiO2 composite material that is responsive to visible light for glucose photoreforming. When TiO2 is coupled with CQDs, the light absorption capacity of the CQDs/TiO2 hybrid system is significantly enhanced, especially in the visible light region. Despite the excellent performance of CQDs, it has rarely been reported that CQDs has been applied to photocatalytic formate dehydrogenation systems. Considering that in many formate dehydrogenation catalysts, carbon materials are often used as the carrier of Pd nanoparticles, and carbon materials have good stability under acidic conditions such as formic acid, it is feasible to apply CQDs in photocatalytic formic acid dehydrogenation system.

Herein, mesoporous TiO2 continues to be selected as our previous work [52], and CQDs/TiO2 composite materials are constructed first, followed by –NH2 modification and Pd loading. A series of Pd/CQDs/TiO2–NH2 photocatalysts were synthesized, which were further used as photocatalysts for formic acid dehydrogenation. The effects of CQDs deposition on the microstructure, photoelectric properties and photocatalytic formic acid dehydrogenation properties were explored. Coupling CQDs with TiO2 to form a heterojunction can improve the efficiency of light absorption.
For the details of preparation of Pd/CQDs/TiO2–NH2 composite schottky catalyst, catalysts characterization, photocatalytic performance test, and etc., please see Electronic Supplementary Material.

Scheme 1 provides an outline of the fabrication method for Pd/CQDs/TiO2–NH2. To produce the CQDs/TiO2 blend, mix 0.5 g of TiO2 into 20 mL of CQDs solution (20 mg/mL) and allow it to stir at room temperature for 20 min. Transfer the mixture into a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and subject it to heating in water at 140 °C for 1 h. Once the heating is complete, separate the reactants and wash them by centrifugation with deionized water and ethanol until the resulting liquid is completely transparent and devoid of any color. Finally, the obtained samples were dried in a 60 °C oven overnight to obtain CQDs-1/TiO2 composites. Since the deposition time has a strong influence on the formation of CQDs/TiO2 heterojunction, according to this method, CQDS-x/TiO2 with different deposition time (where x = 0.5, 1, 2, unit is h) was synthesized, respectively. These are named as CQDS-0.5/TiO2, CQDS-1/TiO2, CQDS-2/TiO2. CQDs/TiO2–NH2 was synthesized self-assembly methods using (3-aminopropyl) triethoxysilane (APTES) as the precursor. The as-prepared CQDs/TiO2–NH2 was immobilized Pd nanoparticle to synthesize Pd/CQDs/TiO2–NH2 using wet-impregnation method.

Fig. 1a, b displays the dimensions of CQDs captured by transmission electron microscopy (TEM).The particle size of CQDs synthesized by solid phase thermal polymerization is concentrated in 4–8 nm, indicating the successful preparation of CQDs. In order to confirm the successful deposition of CQDs on TiO2, STEM tests were carried out on CQDS-1/TiO2, but surprisingly, no spots of 4–8 nm were found as shown in Fig. 1 c. This may be because there is no contrast difference between CQDs and TiO2, which can not be clearly distinguished. Further characterization is needed to prove whether CQDs was successfully deposited on TiO2. However, after loading Pd, Pd nanoparticles can be easily observed through STEM testing. As shown in Fig. 1d, in the Pd/CQDs-1/TiO2–NH2 catalyst, Pd nanoparticles are evenly dispersed on CQDs-1/TiO2–NH2, with an average particle size of about 1.49 nm. Compared with Pd/TiO2–NH2, the size of Pd nanoparticles is smaller, which may be due to the rich functional groups on the surface of CQDs. And this is conducive to the dispersion of Pd nanoparticles. As shown in Fig. 1e, the element distribution image confirms the presence of Ti and Pd elements, and Pd elements are evenly distributed on CQDs-1/TiO2–NH2.

The XRD patterns of Pd/TiO2–NH2, Pd/CQDs-0.5/TiO2–NH2, Pd/CQDs-1/TiO2–NH2 and Pd/CQDs-2/TiO2–NH2 are shown in Fig. 2 a. The above four photocatalysts exhibit similar diffraction peaks, and Pd/TiO–NH2 exhibits the peak of anatase TiO2. It can be observed that 25.28, 37.8, 48.05, 53.89, 55.06, 62.69, 68.76, 70.31 and 74.03° can be indexed to the (101), (004), (200), (105), (211), (204), (116), (220) and (107) facets of anatase-phase structured TiO2 [27,41]. However, the diffraction peak positions for various Pd/CQDs/TiO2–NH2 catalysts remained largely unchanged, suggesting that the incorporation of CQDs onto TiO2 does not alter the crystal phase of TiO2. This stability might be attributed to the amorphous nature and even distribution of CQDs on the TiO2 surface. However, the peak intensity of Pd/CQDs-2/TiO2–NH2 decreased slightly, which may be due to the prolonged deposition of CQDs on the hydrothermal surface, which reduced the crystallinity of TiO2. Moreover, the XRD results for the four photocatalysts showed no detectable peaks corresponding to Pd. This could be because Pd is either too well-distributed to show up or present in such small amounts that it falls below the detection threshold.

FT-IR spectra for TiO2, CQDs/TiO2, and Pd/CQDs-1/TiO2–NH2 are shown in Fig. 2b. Each of these samples exhibits a peak around 1631 cm⁻1, which is associated with the Ti–OH stretching vibration [53]. After deposition of CQDs on TiO2, two new characteristic absorption peaks are shown at the band of 2280–2390 cm−1, which belong to the stretching vibration of cyanocyanide C N, which proves that the synthesized CQDs contains nitrogen. In addition, the characteristic peaks of Pd/CQDs-1/TiO2–NH2 at 1214 cm−1 and 1155 cm−1 correspond to Si–O–Si stretching vibration and Ti–O–Si stretching vibration, respectively.

Fig. 2c, d present the N₂ adsorption-desorption isotherms and pore size distribution for Pd/TiO₂-NH₂ and Pd/CQDs-1/TiO2-NH2. Notably, the isotherms for both materials display clear hysteresis within the relative pressure span of 0.5–1.0, suggesting a characteristic type IV isotherm pattern. The SBET of Pd/CQDs-1/TiO2–NH2 and Pd/TiO2–NH2 are 104.8 m2g-1 and 112.6 m2g-1, respectively, indicating a slight decrease in specific surface area after deposition of CQDs. Additionally, the pore size distribution data indicate that the mean pore diameter for Pd/TiO₂-NH₂ is approximately 9.2 nm, whereas for Pd/CQDs-1/TiO₂-NH₂, it is about 6.0 nm. The results show that the pore sizes become significantly smaller after the introduction of CQDs, which indicates that some CQDs is deposited in the pore channels of TiO2. The pore structure data obtained based on N2 absorption and desorption isotherm analysis are shown in Table 1 . It can be found that with the increase of deposition time of CQDs, the specific surface area, pore volume and pore diameter continue to decrease, but the decrease is not large, and the effect on the photocatalytic formic acid dehydrogenation activity is not significant.

XPS provides information on the elemental composition, valency and chemical environment of the Pd/CQDs-1/TiO2–NH2 surface. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the full spectrum shows the presence of elements such as C, N, Ti, O, Si and Pd on the surface of Pd/CQDs-1/TiO2–NH2. The C 1s spectrum depicted in Fig. 3b reveals that CQDs-1/TiO₂ displays peaks at 284.8 eV, 286.2 eV, and 288.9 eV. These features represent the presence of carbon-carbon (C–C/CC), carbon-oxygen (C–O), and carbonyl (CO) groups [54,55]. However, CQDs-1/TiO2–NH2 and Pd/CQDs-1/TiO2–NH2 did not have obvious peaks at 288.9 eV, indicating that CO disappeared after grafting APTES, which may be because some oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of CQDs reacted with APTES.

According to Fig. 3c, the N 1s spectrum for CQDs-1/TiO2 displays a peak at 400.0 eV corresponding to C N, which matches the findings from FT-IR analysis. While CQDs-1/TiO2–NH2 has two obvious characteristic peaks at 401.1 eV and 399.3 eV, corresponding to –NH3 + and –NH2 bonds respectively, but no obvious peaks are found near 400.0 eV, which may be because after grafting APTES, the proportion of C N of CQDs becomes smaller and the signal strength becomes weaker. In addition, Pd/CQDs-1/TiO2–NH2 and Pd/TiO2–NH2 both have two characteristic peaks at 401.6eV and 399.7eV, corresponding to –NH3 + and –NH2 bonds, respectively. Compared with CQDs-1/TiO2–NH2, the N 1s peaks of Pd/CQDs-1/TiO2–NH2 and Pd/TiO2–NH2 are shifted in the direction of higher binding energy, which indicates the interaction between –NH2 and Pd. However, Fig. 3d indicates that the Pd 3d spectra for both Pd/CQDs-1/TiO2–NH2 and Pd/TiO2–NH2 are essentially the same. This implies that the presence of CQDs does not notably influence the oxidation state or the chemical environment of Pd. The primary peaks at 335.2 eV and 340.5 eV correspond to Pd⁰ (3d₅/₂ and 3d₃/₂, respectively), whereas the less prominent peaks at 337.4 eV and 342.5 eV are attributed to Pd2⁺ (3d₅/₂ and 3d₃/₂, respectively). Table 2 presents the palladium levels determined by ICP-OES, which closely match the expected values, confirming the accuracy of the theoretical estimates.

The photocatalytic performance is primarily governed by the material's optical characteristics as well as the efficiency of electron separation and migration. As mentioned above, CQDs has good light absorption properties, so in order to explore the changes of light absorption properties and band structure of photocatalyst after deposition of CQDs, UV–vis DRS tests were carried out on Pd/TiO2–NH2, Pd/CQDs-0.5/TiO2–NH2, Pd/CQDs-1/TiO2–NH2, Pd/CQDs-2/TiO2–NH2, and the results were shown in Fig. 4 a. Compared with Pd/TiO2–NH2, The light absorption intensity of Pd/CQDs-0.5/TiO2–NH2 and Pd/CQDs-1/TiO2–NH2 increased in the visible region, confirming the role of CQDs. Among them, Pd/CQDs-1/TiO2–NH2 has the strongest visible light absorption, which indicates that Pd/CQDs-1/TiO2–NH2 composites can absorb more sunlight and generate more photogenerated electron-hole pairs for reaction, thereby improving the photocatalytic activity of formic acid dehydrogenation. However, with the further increase of deposition time, the light absorption intensity of Pd/CQDs-2/TiO2–NH2 in the visible region decreased, which may be due to the accumulation of CQDs with the increase of deposition time, the loss of excellent optical properties, and too much CQDs blocked the bulk phase of TiO2, thus inhibiting the light absorption of TiO2. In addition, in the ultraviolet region, the light absorption of Pd/CQDs-0.5/TiO2–NH2 is the strongest, while Pd/CQDs-1/TiO2–NH2 and Pd/CQDs-2/TiO2–NH2 decline in turn, which may be related to the masking effect and upconversion characteristics of CQDs [56]. In addition, as shown in Fig. 4b, the band gap (E) of Pd/TiO2–NH2, Pd/CQDs-0.5/TiO2–NH2, Pd/CQDs-1/TiO2–NH2, Pd/CQDs-2/TiO2–NH2 is 3.12 eV, 3.04 eV, 2.93 eV and 2.82 eV, respectively, calculated by Tauc plot method. This indicates that the introduction of CQDs helps to reduce the band gap of TiO2-based photocatalysts.

Fig. 4c presents the Mott-Schottky analysis of Pd/CQDs-1/TiO2–NH2 and Pd/TiO2–NH2. Both curves exhibit positive slopes, consistent with N-type semiconductors. The increased slope of Pd/CQDs-1/TiO2–NH2 suggests it has a diminished carrier density relative to Pd/TiO2–NH2. Calculations for carrier density and flat band potential (E)fb were derived from the Mott-Schottky curve. As shown in Table 3 , Pd/CQDs-1/TiO2–NH2 exhibits a carrier density of 2.72 × 1020 cm⁻³, which is less than the 3.55 × 1020 cm⁻³ measured for Pd/TiO2–NH2. This indicates that the corrosion resistance of the photocatalyst is enhanced after the introduction of CQDs. Compared with Pd/TiO2–NH2, Efb of Pd/CQDs-1/TiO2–NH2 moves forward, which indicates that the band edge bending of Pd/CQDs-1/TiO2–NH2 is smaller, which is conducive to the transfer of electrons with aqueous solution.

The characteristics of electron separation and migration in photocatalysts are evaluated via EIS, photocurrent response, and PL spectra. As depicted in Fig. 4d, the EIS curves of Pd/CQDs-1/TiO2–NH2 and Pd/TiO2–NH2 display comparable circular radii. This indicates that the introduction of CQDs has no significant effect on the electronic conductivity of Pd/TiO2–NH2 based catalysts. Fig. 4e illustrates the use of I-t curve measurements to explore how charge carriers transfer in Pd/CQDs-1/TiO2–NH2 and Pd/TiO2–NH2 when exposed to light. Pd/CQDs-1/TiO2–NH2 composites show a photocurrent density of around 0.4 μA cm⁻2 when exposed to visible light, compared to about 0.22 μA cm⁻2 for Pd/TiO2–NH2. This indicates that the Pd/CQDs-1/TiO2–NH2 material has enhanced light response and a lower rate of electron-hole recombination. CQDs improve the efficiency of electron separation and transport in photocatalytic processes, resulting in reduced recombination rates. However, extended light exposure leads to a gradual reduction in photocurrent, possibly because CQDs degrade or lose effectiveness when exposed to light for longer periods. With the continuous illumination of light, the transfer rate of CQDs to electrons gradually weakens, and the separation efficiency of photogenerated electrons and photogenerated holes gradually decreases. These results indicate that Pd/CQDs-1/TiO2–NH2 can effectively separate and transfer photogenerated electron-hole pairs, thereby improving the photocatalytic activity, but the stability still needs to be improved.

To gauge how effectively photogenerated electron-hole pairs are separated, PL spectroscopy was employed. Generally, increased fluorescence intensity suggests that the photocatalyst is less effective at separating these charge carriers. Fig. 4f illustrates that Pd/CQDs-1/TiO2–NH2 exhibits a lower fluorescence peak intensity compared to Pd/TiO2–NH2, with a shorter fluorescence emission wavelength. This suggests that a heterojunction has formed between Pd, CQDs, and TiO2. The integration of these three components enhances charge mobility at their junction, significantly minimizing the recombination of electron-hole pairs generated by light. The extension of electron-hole pair lifetime enables more photogenerated carriers to participate in the reaction, thus improving the hydrogen production efficiency of photocatalyzed formic acid decomposition.

As depicted in Fig. 5 a, the efficiency of hydrogen generation from formic acid solution at 308 K under visible light is compared among Pd/TiO2–NH2, Pd/CQDs-0.5/TiO2–NH2, Pd/CQDs-1/TiO2–NH2, and Pd/CQDs-2/TiO2–NH2 catalysts. Pd/CQDs/TiO2–NH2 catalysts with different CQDs deposition times all have better performance than Pd/TiO2–NH2 catalysts, indicating that after the introduction of CQDs, the photocatalytic performance has been significantly improved, and the improvement of catalytic performance can be attributed to the improvement of visible light absorption rate, the further reduction of Pd NPs size and the higher photogenerated electron-hole pair separation efficiency. Pd/CQDs-1/TiO2–NH2 showed the best hydrogen production performance among all catalysts. 200 mL of gas could be generated within 12 min, the hydrogen precipitation rate reached 19.8 mmol h−1, and the formic acid was almost completely decomposed.

Hydrogen production from formic acid was examined under both illuminated and non-illuminated conditions to evaluate the effect of light. Fig. 5b, c highlights that the catalytic activity of Pd/TiO2–NH2 and Pd/CQDs-1/TiO2–NH2 is markedly enhanced under illumination compared to dark conditions. Moreover, in the dark, Pd/CQDs-1/TiO2–NH2 surpasses Pd/TiO2–NH2 in catalytic efficiency, proving its superior performance. The formic acid dehydrogenation is significantly influenced by the size of Pd nanoparticles. With light exposure, the TOF for Pd/CQDs-1/TiO2–NH2 is 2666.1 h−1, which is higher than the 2317.2 h−1 recorded without light. This performance outshines the majority of reported Pd-based heterogeneous catalysts, as evidenced in Table 4 [57–64]. Studying the catalyst's cyclic stability is vital for its future use. As depicted in Fig. 5d, the H2 yield of the Pd/CQDs-1/TiO2–NH2 catalyst retains over 80% of its original value even after four cycles. The decrease in activity may be due to the presence of enlarged Pd nanoparticles during the photoinduced reaction and the instability of CQDs to light, as can be demonstrated in Fig. 4e and 6 . The size distribution of Pd nanoparticles in recycled Pd/CQDs-1/TiO2–NH2 is 1.93 ± 0.9 nm, is larger than that of the initial catalyst, thereby leading to the decrease in activity. This result is consistent with previous reports in the literature [65,66].

Fig. 7 a demonstrates that introducing a NaOH trap reduces the gas output by 50%, suggesting an equimolar relationship between H2 and CO2. Additionally, Fig. 7b reveals that without CO2 generation, the ratio of H2 to CO remains 1:1 in this reaction. These results verified that although the addition of CQDs greatly improved the photocatalytic formic acid decomposition performance, no dehydration reaction occurred. Combined with the characterization and photocatalysis results, it is concluded that the main reasons for the improved photocatalytic performance of Pd/CQDs-1/TiO2–NH2 compared with Pd/TiO2–NH2 are that smaller Pd nanoparticles expose more active sites, higher visible light absorption rate and better photogenerated electron-hole separation efficiency.

Combined with the optical and electrochemical characterization and photocatalytic results, the possible mechanism of photocatalyzing FA to produce hydrogen on Pd/CQDs-1/TiO2–NH2 is proposed as shown in Fig. 8 a. When CQDs is loaded on TiO2, a heterojunction is formed. In visible light, CQDs and TiO2–NH2 can be effectively excited by photons with enough energy to produce electrons and holes from conduction band (CB) and valence band (VB), respectively. The transfer of electrons involves two pathways: (1) Because the CB position of CQDs is more negative than the CB position of TiO2–NH2, an internal local electric field is generated, which makes the electrons on CB of CQDs quickly transfer to CB of TiO2–NH2, and because the active site Pd forms a Schottky barrier with the carrier. Electrons from the TiO2–NH2 conduction band traverse the Schottky barrier and are captured by Pd. The Pd nanoparticles, now holding these electrons, can further reduce the adsorbed H+ into hydrogen; (2) The electrons in CQDs CB can also be transferred directly to Pd NPs. On the other hand, since the VB position of TiO2–NH2 is more corrected than that of CQDs, the holes created in TiO2–NH2 VB can be easily transferred to the VB of CQDs, which in turn reacts with HCOOH to form CO2 and H2. The formation of this composite structure enables efficient charge separation and transfer. The dual optical absorption of CQDs and TiO2–NH2 and the improvement of interfacial charge transfer and separation in heterojunction significantly improve the photoelectrochemical properties and formate dehydrogenation properties of Pd/CQDs-1/TiO2–NH2. Therefore, compared with Pd/TiO2–NH2, Pd/CQDs-1/TiO2–NH2 has significantly higher photocatalytic activity.

The interaction between the highly conductive CQDs and the electron-absorbing Pd nanoparticles improved the band structure (illustrated in Fig. 8b with measured VB and CB potentials), boosted visible light absorption, and created electron/hole capture sites. These changes significantly expedited electron/hole transfer and separation, thereby enhancing the utilization of photogenerated electrons and holes [54,55]. Consequently, this synergy greatly increased the photocatalytic H2 production efficiency of Pd/CQDs-1/TiO2–NH2.

In this work, CQDs were successfully deposited on mesoporous TiO₂ using a hydrothermal method, producing a series of CQDs/TiO2 heterojunction materials with varying CQDs deposition times. Subsequently, a range of Pd/CQDs/TiO2–NH2 three-component Schottky photocatalysts were constructed through chemical modification and wet-impregnation methods, and these were applied to the photocatalytic FA dehydrogenation reaction. Among them, the Pd/CQDs-1/TiO2–NH2 catalyst exhibited the best photocatalytic FA dehydrogenation performance under visible light at 308 K, achieving a TOF of 2666.1 h−1, significantly higher than Pd/TiO2–NH2 (1715.5 h−1) and 1.15 times the TOF (2317.2 h−1) without light. In addition, Pd/CQDs-1/TiO2–NH2 maintained good photocatalytic activity after four cycles. Compared to Pd/TiO2–NH2, Pd/CQDs-1/TiO2–NH2 composites exhibited better visible light absorption characteristics, indicating that an appropriate amount of CQDs can improve sunlight utilization. However, excessive CQD deposition may cause accumulation, hindering TiO2 light absorption and weakening overall composite light absorption. Furthermore, the Pd/CQDs/TiO2–NH2 three-component heterojunction structure promotes the separation and transfer of photogenerated carriers, thus the deposition of an appropriate amount of CQDs can effectively enhance the efficiency of photocatalytic formic acid decomposition and hydrogen production. This work provides new insights for the synthesis of composite catalysts for green hydrogen energy applications using CQDs.

Fengqiu Chen: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Qiu Wang: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Shengda Lin: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation. Huanhu Luo: Methodology, Investigation. Wanjin Yu: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision. Wucan Liu: Methodology, Investigation. Dang-guo Cheng: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21622606), and the Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (LZ23B060001).
